nativeRange.habitat: The Calvus inhabits the rocky littoral zone of Lake Tanganyika, clinging to steep boulder-strewn slopes and crevice-rich cliff faces from shallow inshore areas down to deeper rocky drop-offs. The water throughout this zone is highly oxygenated, clear, hard, and strongly alkaline — among the most chemically stable lake environments on Earth.

decorPreferences: large piles of rocks with narrow crevices and caves
empty snail shells (Neothauma tanganyicense or alternatives) for female spawning sites
sandy substrate between rock formations for shell-dweller colonies
minimal planting — hardy species such as Anubias or Vallisneria if desired
ample open rock face for foraging

diet.notes: The Calvus is a predatory piscivore and invertivore in the wild, feeding by inserting its narrow, laterally compressed head into rock crevices to prey on small fish, fry, and invertebrates. In captivity it generally accepts quality frozen foods such as brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, bloodworms, and krill, along with small cichlid pellets and carnivore sticks. Live foods can be offered to encourage feeding in newly acquired specimens that are reluctant to take prepared food. The Calvus does not graze algae. Feed sparingly — this is a slow-metabolism, slow-growing species, and overfeeding degrades water quality rapidly.

goodWith: shell-dwellers (Lamprologus ocellatus, Neolamprologus multifasciatus, N. similis) — natural Lake Tanganyika community
other Lake Tanganyika rock-dwellers of similar or larger size (Julidochromis, Lepidiolamprologus attenuatus)
Lake Tanganyika goby cichlids (Eretmodus, Spathodus, Tanganicodus)
Synodontis petricola and other Lake Tanganyika catfish
avoidWith: small fish or fry that fit in its mouth — will be eaten
shell-dweller fry and eggs (calvus actively raids shell broods as natural predator)
soft-water or acid-water cichlids (Amazonian, West African)
fast-moving boisterous fish that outcompete at feeding time
conspecific males in small tanks without visual barriers

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by 5snake5; 4124x2749. Tack-sharp full-body lateral view showing the extreme lateral compression, steep concave forehead, and vivid pearly blue-white spotting across flanks and fins — all key diagnostic features on a vibrant live specimen.

Photo by Jan Sevcik (no known copyright restrictions / public domain); 750x500. Sharp dark black-form adult beside a snail shell showing natural crevice/shell behaviour and pearl spotting; modest corner watermark.

summary: The Calvus is a Lake Tanganyika predator with an extraordinarily laterally compressed body that allows it to wedge into rock crevices and raid shell-dweller nests. One of the slowest-growing cichlids in the hobby, it takes 3–5 years to reach full size. Stable, hard alkaline water (pH 8.2–9.0) and considerable patience are required.
careNotes:
Altolamprologus calvus is one of the most laterally compressed cichlids in existence — its razor-thin body allows it to wedge into the narrowest rock crevices to hunt small fish, fry, and crustaceans in its native Lake Tanganyika. This extreme lateral compression is what distinguishes calvus functionally from most cichlids, and it directly enables the species' signature behavior: raiding the shell-broods of shell-dwelling Lamprologines.

Species confusion: A. calvus is frequently confused with its sibling species A. compressiceps. The key morphological distinction is the forehead profile — calvus has a steeper, more concave ("hollow") dorsal head profile, giving the snout an almost upturned appearance when viewed from the side. A. compressiceps has a straighter or slightly convex dorsal slope from the nape to the snout tip. Both species share the same laterally compressed body shape, overlapping colour forms, and similar care requirements, but they are separate species with non-overlapping wild distributions within the lake.

Colour forms: A. calvus is available in several geographic colour forms: Black (most common in trade; from southern Congolese localities), White/Pearl (pale body with dark margins), Yellow (golden-yellow body colouration), and Inkfin (dark fins with contrasting pale body). These are distinct collection localities, not captive morphs, and should not be cross-bred.

Growth rate: Among the slowest-growing cichlids in the hobby. Under ideal conditions, a juvenile purchased at 3–4 cm may take 2–3 years to reach sexual maturity and a further 1–2 years to attain full adult size (males to ~15 cm, females remaining smaller at ~8–10 cm). Rapid growth from overfeeding should be avoided; it can distort body shape and stress the fish.

Water chemistry: Lake Tanganyika is one of the world's most chemically stable lakes — temperature, pH, hardness, and mineral content fluctuate very little in the wild. Replicate this stability in captivity. Target pH 8.2–9.0 with hard, well-buffered water (kH 8–18, gH 10–20). Use Tanganyika-specific mineral buffers or crush coral/aragonite substrate to maintain alkalinity. Avoid large, rapid water changes; 10–15% weekly with pre-conditioned water is preferable to 25–30% biweekly.

Community keeping: Calvus is an effective predator of small fish but is not broadly aggressive toward unrelated tankmates of comparable or larger size. A classic Tanganyikan biotope community pairs calvus with shell-dwellers — the natural predator-prey relationship plays out in a way that mirrors the lake ecology, with the shell-dwellers at low risk when provided adequate shells, and the calvus benefiting from the visual interest and protein opportunity. This combination is widely recommended by Tanganyikan cichlid specialists.

Feeding: Feed carnivore-appropriate frozen and prepared foods 2–3 times per week in modest portions. Overfeeding is a common mistake; calvus has low metabolic demands relative to its body size. New specimens may refuse prepared foods initially — offer live or frozen brine shrimp and mysis to establish feeding response before transitioning to pellets.

Sexing: Males grow significantly larger (to ~15 cm) than females (~8–10 cm); mature males develop more pronounced forehead scaling and nuchal hump. Pairs can be difficult to form outside of juvenile groups; purchasing a group of 4–6 juveniles and allowing pair formation naturally is recommended.
breedingNotes: The Calvus is a substrate cave spawner. In captivity the female deposits eggs inside an empty snail shell (Neothauma or a suitable substitute) or within a narrow rock crevice, brooding the eggs and wrigglers alone while the male defends the broader territory. Clutch size is small — typically 20–80 eggs — reflecting the confined brooding environment. Fry are extremely small at first and require infusoria or commercial fry foods before they are large enough to accept baby brine shrimp. Development is slow; fry may take six months or more to reach 2 cm. Breeding is considered difficult because it demands stable, species-appropriate water chemistry, an appropriately sized shell or crevice, and a genuinely compatible established pair — something that is difficult to engineer artificially and is best achieved by purchasing a group of juveniles and allowing a pair to form naturally. Separating fry from the male, who may consume them once the female's brood-guarding breaks down, tends to improve survival rates.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Altolamprologus calvus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Lake Tanganyika rocky littoral, DRC/Zambia), depth range, water parameters (pH 8.0–9.0, temperature 24–26°C, hardness 8–25 dH), adult size (males to 14 cm, females ~8 cm), diet (invertivore/piscivore), tank requirements, compatibility notes, and breeding behaviour (shell/crevice spawner, female brooder). Notes species confusion with A. compressiceps and distinguishes by forehead profile.

additional.name: FishBase — Altolamprologus calvus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), adult TL (males to 15 cm), native range (Lake Tanganyika, Zambia/Congo), depth range (3–40 m reported), temperature (23–27°C), and IUCN status. Confirmed lifespan estimates aligned with cichlid literature. Distribution map confirms restricted endemic range within Lake Tanganyika rocky southern and western shores.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Cichlid Blog Reference (Altolamprologus)
additional.notes: Aquarium Co-Op blog/care reference covering Altolamprologus genus. Confirmed trade availability of Black, White, Yellow, and Inkfin colour forms; tank size recommendation (55+ gal / ~208 L cited for a community; 40 gal / 150 L acceptable for a pair); feeding notes (frozen carnivore foods); slow growth rate warning; and community pairing advice with shell-dwellers.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Lake Tanganyika Cichlid Profile: Altolamprologus calvus
additional.notes: Supplementary source. Confirmed lateral compression as predatory adaptation for crevice invasion, shell-dweller predator-prey community keeping notes, colour form descriptions (Black, Yellow, White, Inkfin), species distinction from A. compressiceps (forehead slope), and breeding notes (small clutch, slow fry development, female cave guard). pH 8.2–9.0 and water stability requirements confirmed.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Altolamprologus: The Compressiceps Complex
additional.notes: TFH reference covering the Altolamprologus genus including calvus vs compressiceps identification guide. Confirmed snout profile as primary field-identification character (calvus = steeper/concave, compressiceps = straighter dorsal slope), geographic colour forms as separate collection localities not to be interbred, lifespan estimates (8–12+ years with proper care), and advanced care designation due to water chemistry demands and slow growth.

fish.breedingNotes: The female deposits eggs inside an empty snail shell or a narrow rock crevice and broods the clutch alone, while the male guards the surrounding territory. Clutches are small, typically 20–80 eggs. Fry are tiny at hatching and develop slowly, requiring infusoria or fine fry foods before graduating to baby brine shrimp. Pair formation tends to be challenging unless the fish have been raised together from juveniles. Water chemistry must remain stable and species-appropriate throughout the spawning and rearing period.
